3.why: We will fill it only after the links have been rebuilt in reversed order.
3.invariant: No reversed output values have been produced yet.

4.happening: The input values are copied into node storage.
4.why: The visualizer treats each index as one linked-list node.
4.invariant: Every node keeps the same value throughout the algorithm.

5.why: The count tells us how many link slots are needed.
5.invariant: The list size never changes; only next pointers change.

6.happening: Every node's next link is initialized to -1.
6.why: -1 means there is no next node, so links start safe before the forward chain is built.
6.invariant: All link entries are valid sentinels or valid node IDs.

7.why: The original chain is built from left to right.
7.invariant: Links before i have already been prepared.

8.happening: Node {i} still has a node after it.
8.why: A non-tail node should point forward to the next index.
8.invariant: The original list is being built as 0 -> 1 -> 2 and so on.

8.happening: There are no more forward links to create.
8.why: The last node should keep -1 as its next pointer.
8.invariant: The original linked list is now represented by nodes and links.

9.why: This recreates the original singly linked list.
9.invariant: Each prepared node points to its original next node.

10.happening: i moves from {i} to the next index.
10.why: The next link slot can now be prepared.
10.invariant: All links before the new i are correct.

11.why: A stack returns nodes in last-in, first-out order, which naturally reverses traversal order.
11.invariant: The stack contains visited node IDs, not new nodes.

12.happening: current starts at the head node.
12.why: The first traversal must visit the original list from head to tail.
12.invariant: current is either a valid node ID or -1.

13.why: There is no node 0 to visit, so current must become -1.
13.invariant: An empty list should remain empty after reversal.

13.happening: The input list has at least one node.
13.why: Node 0 is a valid starting head.
13.invariant: current can safely point at node 0.

14.happening: current is set to -1 for the empty list.
14.why: This prevents any attempt to follow a node that does not exist.
14.invariant: No traversal happens when the list has no nodes.

15.happening: No changed link is highlighted yet.
15.why: The first pass only pushes nodes; it does not reverse pointers.
15.invariant: The original links are still intact.

16.why: This node must be remembered so it can be popped later in reverse order.
16.invariant: All nodes already passed are stored in the stack.

16.happening: current reached -1, so the first traversal is complete.
16.why: Every original node has been pushed onto the stack.
16.invariant: The stack now stores the original list from head to tail.

17.happening: Node {current} is pushed onto the stack.
17.why: Later, popping will visit tail first and head last.
17.invariant: The stack preserves all visited nodes.

18.happening: current follows its original next pointer.
18.why: This moves the traversal one node closer to the tail.
18.invariant: No links have been changed during this push phase.

19.why: The first popped node will become the head of the reversed list.
19.invariant: No reversed chain has been built yet.

20.why: There is no tail until the reversed chain gets its first node.
20.invariant: tail will always point to the last node of the rebuilt chain once it exists.

21.why: This gives the visual pointer a safe value before any pop occurs.
21.invariant: node will soon hold the popped node ID.

22.happening: The stack still has {len(stack)} node IDs.
22.why: Each pop adds one node to the reversed chain.
22.invariant: Unpopped nodes are exactly the nodes not yet attached in reversed order.

22.why: All nodes have been attached to the reversed chain.
22.invariant: The links now describe the reversed list.

23.happening: The top node is popped from the stack.
23.why: Because the original tail was pushed last, it comes out first.
23.invariant: Popped nodes are processed in reversed order.

24.why: The first popped node must become the new head.
24.invariant: The reversed chain is about to get its first node.

24.why: This popped node should be attached after the current tail.
24.invariant: The chain from new_head to tail is already reversed correctly.

25.why: It was the last node of the original list, so it should be first after reversal.
25.invariant: new_head points to the first node of the reversed list.

26.why: A one-node reversed chain has the same head and tail.
26.invariant: tail marks the end of the reversed chain.

28.happening: The current tail node {tail} is marked for a link update.
28.why: Its next pointer will connect to the newly popped node.
28.invariant: The chain before tail is already correct.

29.happening: tail.next is about to point to node {node}.
29.why: Attaching the popped node extends the reversed chain by one node.
29.invariant: The reversed chain grows in the exact order nodes are popped.

30.why: The newly attached node is now the last node in the reversed chain.
30.invariant: tail always marks the final node of the partial reversed list.

31.happening: The new tail node {tail} is marked.
31.why: Its old next pointer must be cleared so the reversed chain does not accidentally point forward.
31.invariant: The tail of a proper linked list points to -1.

32.why: This cleanly terminates the partial reversed list.
32.invariant: The chain from new_head to tail is valid and reversed.

33.why: The algorithm is done mutating links and is about to read the answer.
33.invariant: The final linked structure is stable.

34.why: Reading values from the new head follows the reversed list.
34.invariant: current is at the next node whose value should be output.

35.happening: current points to node {current} in the reversed list.
35.why: Its value should be appended to the answer.
35.invariant: result contains the reversed values already visited.

35.why: Every node in the reversed list has been read.

36.happening: The value at node {current} is read.
36.why: The returned answer is shown as values, not node IDs.
36.invariant: The linked-list structure is no longer changing.

37.why: This records the next value in reversed order.
37.invariant: result matches the prefix of the reversed list already traversed.

38.happening: current follows the reversed next pointer.
38.why: This moves to the next node in the rebuilt list.
38.invariant: The traversal follows the reversed chain from head to tail.

39.why: All nodes have been visited in reversed order.
39.invariant: The output contains exactly the original values, reversed.

init.happening: Set up nodes, links, and helper variables.
init.why: The linked list must be represented before it can be traversed or rewired.

loop.happening: Check whether another node or stack item remains.
loop.why: Loops stop exactly at the no-node sentinel or an empty stack.
loop.invariant: Work only proceeds while there is something valid to process.

update.happening: Update the stack, links, tail, or result.
update.why: Each update moves one piece of the reversal forward.
update.invariant: Already processed nodes stay in correct reversed order.

move.happening: Move a pointer to the next relevant node.
move.why: Pointer movement drives traversal through the list.
move.invariant: Pointers either identify real nodes or use -1 for no node.

check.happening: Choose the correct branch for an edge case or first node.
check.why: Empty lists and the first popped node need special handling.
check.invariant: The algorithm never follows an invalid node.

return.why: The reversed linked list has been fully constructed and read.
return.invariant: The final result contains every original node value once.
